data.title: Norwegian Cruise Line: Deep-Value Buy Thesis Remains Compelling - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
NCLH Remains Attractively Valued Despite Its Inherent Underperformance - Buy Its Ongoing Recovery

We previously covered Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) in September 2023, discussing how the management had shown great competency in managing the elevated interest rate risks, attributed to the debt swap to mostly fixed rates by FQ2'23.

Combined with the robust cruise booking trends surpassing pre-COVID levels, the excellent advanced ticket sales, and the increasingly booked position, we had initiated a buy rating then, with the caveat that the stock was only suitable for investors who were patient, attributed to its uncertain path to pre-pandemic profitability.

NCLH 1Y Stock Price

Since then, NCLH has had a robust outperformance at +36.2%, albeit underperforming the wider market at +44.6% and many of its cruise/travel peers in varying degrees.

The cruise company's performance reversal has been prolonged as previously posited, as observed in the management's relatively lacking reiterated FY2025 guidance with net yields of +2.5% YoY, adj EBITDA margin of 37% (+11.3 points YoY/+7.4 from FY2019 levels of 29.6%), and adj EPS of $2.05 (+12.6% YoY/-59.7% from FY2019 levels of $5.09).

Much of NCLH's headwinds are naturally attributed to the still hefty net debt load at -$13.8B (-6.9% QoQ/-5.3% YoY/-111% from FY2019 levels of -$6.54B) and the elevated net-debt-to-adj-EBITDA ratio of 5.66x (compared to 5.27x a quarter ago, 6.3x a year ago, and 3.4x in FY2019).

When compared its direct peers, including Carnival Corporation (CCL) at a net-debt-to-adj-EBITDA ratio of 3.7x in FQ2'25 and Royal Caribbean Group (RCL) at 3.39x in FQ1'25, it is apparent that NCLH's balance sheet recovery has been slower as expected, against the latter's FY2026 "target of reaching the mid-4s."

Much of the debt headwinds are attributed to NCLH's ongoing fleet renewal and capacity growth, with the management aiming to grow from 19.2M passengers in 2019 to 23.4M passengers in 2024 and eventually to 29.7M passengers by 2028, through numerous new ships.

While we have observed a similar fleet capacity growth cadence in both CCL and RCL, along with the increased investments in private island destinations, it is undeniable that NCLH's progress may have been overly aggressive, as observed in the slower balance sheet recovery.

The same has been observed in NCLH's relatively lower Net Yield growth at $279.51 in FQ1'25 (+1.1% YoY/+8.6% from FY2019 levels of $257.20), against CCL at $200.07 in FQ2'25 (+6.4% YoY/+12% from FY2019 levels of $178.59) and RCL at $258.83 in FQ1'25 (+4.7% YoY/+23.9% from FY2019 levels of $208.88).

As a result of these mixed numbers, we can understand why NCLH's stock price performance has underwhelmed indeed, with its reversal to pre-pandemic levels likely to be prolonged.

With the cruise company expected to report their FQ2'25 earnings call on July 31, 2025, investors may want to closely monitor their performance indeed, especially attributed to the relatively underwhelming consumer deposits of $3.9B in FQ1'25 (+2.6% YoY) compared to CCL at $8.5B (+2.4% YoY) and RCL at $6.33B (+4.8% YoY), as NCLH also reports a notable "softening in its 12-month forward booked position."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates have been consistently downgraded, with NCLH expected to chart an impacted top/bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +8.2%/+16.5% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +9.7%/+20.7%, the 5Y historical performance at +8%/-18.6%, and the 5Y pre-pandemic performance at +15.6%/+17.5%, respectively.

NCLH Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has continued to discount NCLH's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 11.21x, compared to its 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 15.44x, the sector median of 17.22x, CCL at 14.51x, and RCL at 22.15x.

Even so, thanks to its cheap FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.21x and the excellent consensus adj EPS 3Y growth estimates at a CAGR of +16.5%, it is undeniable that NCLH remains compelling at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.67x, compared to the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 0.88x, the sector median of 1.81x, CCL at 0.65x, and RCL at 1.08x.

This development implies that NCLH's investment thesis remains highly attractive, with it offering value-oriented investors the chance to invest in the cruise sector, naturally for those who have missed CCL's and RCL's massive stock gains thus far.

So, Is NCLH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

NCLH 5Y Stock Price

For now, NCLH remains well supported in its uptrend recovery line since early 2023, with the stock consistently generating higher highs/higher lows albeit yet to climb out of its COVID-19 rout after the early 2020 meltdown.

NCLH's Seemingly Overbought Levels

Despite NCLH's seemingly overbought levels, it appears that the rally may still have legs based on the historical trends and technical indicators above, with a breakout from current ranges of $23s likely.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $1.72 (+65.3% sequentially) and the currently discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 11.21x, it seems that the stock is trading at a slight premium to our base-case fair-value estimates of $19.20.

Even so, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $2.88, we believe that NCLH continues to offer an excellent upside potential of +39.6% to our base-case long-term price target of $32.20.

Assuming an eventual re-rating nearer to its 5Y pre-pandemic mean of ~15x (nearer to the sector median of ~17x), there remains a near doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $43.20 as well.

Combined with the bullish uptrend support line and the promising technical indicators, we are cautiously reiterating our Buy rating for NCLH, with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding, preferably after a moderate retracement to the 50/100 day moving averages of $19s for an improved margin of safety.

Given the -17% downside from current levels, we reckon some patience may be more prudent in the mean time.

data.title: Netflix Stock: Q2 Earnings Were Much Better Than The Market Thinks
data.description:
The latest earnings report out of Netflix, Inc. (NASDAQ:NFLX) has created surprisingly mixed feelings among analysts, despite a boost to full-year revenue and profit projections and beats on the top and bottom line.

It Wasn't Just The Dollar, It Was The Dollars

The funny thing is that I’ve been somewhat more skeptical about Netflix than the prevailing market consensus for the last year or so - and that has definitely caused me to miss out on some profits on Netflix shares - but I was actually more impressed by the most recent report than the market was. Netflix shares actually fell 1% following the report, while I felt better about Netflix than I had in a while.

The market’s concerns, almost certainly, stem from the currency issue. Historically, the market has quite rightly declined to credit companies for improvements in financials that stem from depreciation of the dollar; such boost to profit are not reflective of any operational improvement by management and have the shelf life of whatever force is driving the dollar down.

But while it is true that Netflix did see some benefit last quarter from the decline of the dollar, that is hardly all that was happening in the report. Indeed, the growth of the UCAN region, about as dollar-independent as you can get, was an almost stunning 15%, returning to the rate of the holiday quarter and far exceeding the 9% of the prior quarter.

The dollar also is not falling everywhere. The single largest impact of the F/X rate was in Latin America, where 9% growth was actually 23% in constant-dollar currency. While that’s not quite enough to cancel out the F/X impact from Europe and Asia, the dollar depreciation boost is far from the only thing driving Netflix revenues up.

Subscriber Growth Seems Clear

I have my own concerns about Netflix, and this report did not fully address them, but I have to say the quarter impressed me. I had thought that, with Paid Sharing already more or less fully penetrated into the base, advertising still a work in progress at best, and the increasing diversion of funds to expensive sports rights instead of broad general entertainment categories, Netflix would be hitting a few bumps in the road by now. So far it isn’t showing up in the data.

Netflix no longer discloses subscriber counts, but it seems almost impossible that it would be hitting these kinds of marks if it wasn’t still attracting significant new subscribers domestically, as well as abroad. Following on the heels of Netflix’s rather large price hike, the retention really has been quite remarkable.

Content Isn't King....But It Sure Helps

Management reaffirmed what it has said many times before, that even their top titles represent less than 1% of total viewing. Netflix Originals notwithstanding, in business terms the company is a platform, not a traditional content creator, by which I mean that the latter does not move the needle nearly like the former does. For this reason, I don’t really see the need to call out the top titles in a given quarter; Netflix is so successful precisely because when one title loses its freshness another is ready to replace it.

Still, there were a few things that might augur well for Netflix’s future content initiatives. First, one of their top titles this past quarter was KPop Demon Hunters, which is an animated film. This comes only a few years after Netflix animation was so down in the dumps that several of its top animation talents were debating jumping ship. One film does not make a trend, but if Netflix can bounce back consistently in this area it will only further grow its appeal to subscribers of all ages.

Secondly, it was noteworthy what programming wasn’t on Netflix’s quarterly top hits list. Specifically, Stranger Things Season 5, one of the few Netflix shows large enough to actually move the needle on its own, is being released in the second half of the year. Even with Squid Game launching just before quarter’s end, a lot of its positive impact probably spilled over into Q3 as well.

Double, Double, Toil...And Trouble?

Management also reaffirmed that it intends to “roughly double” ads revenue this year. Since they don’t tell us what ad revenue actually was last year, putting that number into context is difficult. I am not exactly down on ad revenue, but I don’t list it as a reason to be bullish, either. Most of the time I simply don’t think about ads as much as a lot of Netflix bulls do.

For one thing, if Netflix is “increasingly successful” at advertising, it’s worth remembering how much success has been redefined downwards since it launched. Netflix came out of the gate with perhaps an inflated sense of its own advertising worth, spurred no doubt by the fact in ad-free subscriptions it probably is as special as it thinks it is.

Advertising is another matter, though. While Netflix came out asking for $65 per CPM in 2022, it has more or less cut back to $30 or below since Amazon (AMZN) entered into the game right behind them. This is not so surprising when you consider the disparity not so much in size - Netflix has more than enough market cap that Amazon can’t simply outspend them in - but in data. Amazon has more data on purchase history and intent than just about any other retailer out there - with the possible exception of Costco (COST) - while Netflix has already admitted that its approach to advertising basically consists of separating audiences by age, gender, and….nope, that’s about it.

Divorces Are Expensive

This has been my argument from the beginning. Netflix simply doesn’t have the targeting data necessary to compete in advertising long term. Management has never agreed with me, however, and continues to pour capital into it. This quarter saw confirmation that Netflix’s in-house ad tech stack is now fully up and running, which means that Microsoft (MSFT) will soon be ejected from its perch atop the Netflix ad operation and become just another bidder for its ad slots.

I’m less certain than some that is something to celebrate, however. When Microsoft goes out the door, it will be taking its minimum revenue guarantee for Netflix’s ad operation with it. As with most all of Netflix’s ad operation, we don’t actually know how large this guarantee is; reports from the WSJ at the time however had it as “very large,” which is ludicrously vague but enough from a respectable publication to worry me.

Even if Netflix does double ad money this year, the Microsoft guarantee will last almost through the end of it, which means the real hit from its loss comes next year. Far from continuing to double, 2026 may see ads come to a screeching halt.

My Lingering Concerns

I am also still reticent about Netflix’s “new direction” as a whole. This is a great company, but to my mind the things that made it great the past three years are the same things that made it great the 15 before that; rigorous focus and discipline in developing a general entertainment platform that prioritizes value for money and versatility, with a strong recommendation algorithm supplementing originals production.

The general consensus has shifted, however, in favor of ads, sharing crackdowns and, most risky of all to my mind, sports. The greatest risk to the Netflix thesis is that sports will lead it astray.

The Bill Comes Due Across Town

We are seeing this happening right now at another service, in fact. The same day that Netflix reported its earnings, Comcast (CMCSA) announced that its streaming service, Peacock, would be hiking prices on its ad-supported and ad-free plans by $3 per month. That would be remarkable enough in and of itself, considering even Netflix prefers to raise prices in smaller increments than that and HBO Max - I no longer have to apologize for calling it that, since Warner Brothers (WBD) CEO David Zaslav has officially switched the name back again - still isn’t even sure it can raise prices at all.

But what makes it even more surprising is that Peacock has not exactly been storming up the charts lately. While Comcast did manage to narrowly edge out Warner Brothers on the streaming side, Peacock trails behind even Paramount’s eponymous streaming service, and Roku (ROKU) has moved ahead of it as well. Market leaders Netflix, YouTube, Amazon (AMZN) and Disney (DIS) are so far ahead Peacock’s not even in their rearview mirror.

What’s happening here? Put simply, Comcast had no choice. Ever since the NBA deal was announced, I have been a constant (overly so?) skeptic of the “good news,” insisting that the high price was such an overpayment it might compromise Peacock by requiring them to raise prices on all of Peacock’s subscribers - including the 90% of American households who do not watch the NBA - in order to pay for it.

It now appears that’s precisely what’s happening.

We've Seen This Movie Before

What is most ridiculous about this is that we’ve seen this movie before. The whole reason Netflix became one of the most valuable global media companies in existence is that American pay-TV providers regularly doubled and tripled rights payments to sports leagues, leaving non-sports subscribers with bigger and bigger bills even though their own content was not receiving any such largesse in spending.

Netflix quickly deduced that for far less money, it could buy veritably all the reruns rights of scripted American television, and a lot of movie library deals, offering non-sports subscribers a far, far better deal. The veritable exodus of such subscribers that followed is what gave rise to the phenomenon of cord-cutting.

Now, Comcast is essentially porting that flaw over to streaming. Make no mistake, it can happen again. If too much money is allocated to sports rights, the price delta between streaming services with sports and streaming services without them grows to the point that non-sports subscribers will once again migrate.

Bringing These Two Threads Together

Netflix isn’t spending enough on sports yet for this to happen to them; but what does concern me is that they might, indeed almost certainly will, go out and buy more sports. The key to Netflix’s future is avoiding falling into the same trap that Peacock is already ensnared in. So far, management is saying all the right things, and its sports bets have been suitably mild.

But the end of the Microsoft advertising floor, and the very real danger that it drives a stalling or even a reverse of ad revenue growth, may yet spur greater desperation. Sports is the primary method by which ad dollars are lured to new platforms and formats; if Netflix stock suffers from a decline in ad money pressure on management will grow to dip deeper into that pool.

This is, to my mind, the single biggest risk to Netflix; that it will abandon the playbook that got it here. The company needs to not only continue to maintain the industry leading recommendation engine that has been its bread and butter, it also needs to avoid opening a price delta with other services by overpaying on sports. Even if there are customers willing to pay $30 or more per month for sports that Netflix can acquire, Netflix needs to avoid commitments that would drive non-sports subscribers into the arms of a competitor.

Quantifying the Risk

It is difficult to put numbers on this risk because we don’t know what Netflix is getting in ad money, we don’t know how much of that is Microsoft’s minimum guarantee, and we don’t know exactly what sports deal Netflix is going to do next. So I recognize that this risk factor analysis is somewhat vague, of necessity. I believe, however, that it reflects the reality of the fundamental dynamics that will govern Netflix the next few years.

To be honest, I wish we had the Netflix of a few years ago back; considering that Netflix was already rebounding even before it launched I wonder if even management, in the quiet of their own thoughts, wishes they had the Great Netflix Correction aftermath to do over again. Was it really necessary to take the most successful media company of the 21st century and turn its non-sports, non-live, ad-free business model completely upside down? Neither Paid Sharing, nor advertising money, nor sports deals have really excited me.

Nevertheless, I would not call this quarter an empty or mere F/X-driven one. The simple fact is, Netflix continues to defy already high expectations. Even in its most mature market, UCAN, revenue growth continues apace. The second half of the year will probably be even stronger than the first, Netflix’s sports deals so far have not been detrimental - Drew Brees will join Netflix’s NFL Christmas coverage this year and the company seems determined to outshine even its Beyonce halo of 2024 - and its industry-leading recommendation algorithm is still generating healthy engagement.

Investment Summary

I took a long position in Netflix right after it crashed in 2022 and rode it all the way up to $720. I ended my long there, foolishly as it turns out. I am not entirely certain the entire run up since then is justified, but there is no denying I got out too soon.

I found myself seriously debating resuming a long position, but the lack of excitement in the market to another strong quarter suggests that just maybe the top is approaching. I am still worried about what the future of this new direction will bring - sports and ads, to my mind, do not play to Netflix’s strengths.

I cannot deny I went neutral too soon, but I am going to stay at a Hold on Netflix.

It was a great quarter, though.

data.title: Saab AB (publ) 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Saab AB (publ) in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Why The Ethereum Complex Stands To Win
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for US recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice and nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

This Time With Real Utility

Whether you are a crypto-skeptic, crypto-native, or like most people merely crypto-curious, I believe now is the time to get smart about the Ethereum complex.

Don’t switch off. This is important, and not because of NFTs, Bored Apes, Lambos or any of that nonsense.

It’s important because the securities industry is embracing Ethereum as a fundamental platform, and I think that it is serious about doing so, and I think there is a good deal of money to be made by investing behind that trend.

Tokenization

You may have heard of ‘tokenization’ and it may sound redundant, scammy, or both. It’s not. Tokenization is a paradigm shift in the asset management industry. Today, tradable securities all reside within set of financial institutions that have existed for decades, more than a century in some cases. Brokerages, clearing houses, custodians, all these elements of the financial plumbing are set up to deal with common stocks issued by joint-stock companies; with ETFs issued by fund managers; with options created from those stocks and ETFs; and with futures created by market-makers enabling real-world producers and traders alike to hedge their exposures. Taken together this is one giant pool of liquidity.

Liquidity, though, is the gift that keeps on giving. If you are an asset manager, a broker, a market-maker, an investor, an asset owner, any part of the chain - you always want more liquidity. And the next source of liquidity is to open up private pools of assets to cash that cannot, today, invest in those pools. You’ve seen the news about 401k plans being opened up to invest in private funds - this is one part of the trend.

One of the primary ways this will happen is that currently non-traded assets, those that do not fit easily within the financial plumbing of public markets, have to be represented in a way that can mimic the common-stock complex. This is tokenization. A “token” is simply a digital representation of part of an asset (ie. a part claim on the asset - like a common stock) or some other right as regards the asset. Let’s say the French state decided to monetize the Palace of Versailles. The Palace could be tokenized by creating 1m part-shares in the Palace itself, claims on ownership like a common stock; and/or by creating 10m memberships of the Palace, entitling the holder to one free visit per year. These rights - a claim on the asset, and the opportunity to visit the asset without further cost - could be combined in a single token. Tokens of this nature offer far more flexibility to the asset owner than do common stocks. Tokens may enable the asset owner to monetize an asset without surrendering any ownership whatsoever. (Tokenization is great news for asset owners.)

Tokenization needs a database of record where the rights and obligations afforded to each token, and the ownership of each token, can be recorded. And the database of choice for this is going to be, in the main, the Ethereum blockchain. Why? Because this particular blockchain was built with “smart contracts” (also known as “contracts” for non-crypto-bros) in mind from day one.

In the 1980s if you had a big dataset to run and manage, you likely paid AT&T to run it on the Tuxedo transaction processing system, and you paid them in U.S. Dollars.

In the 2020s-2030s, with a big dataset of tokens to run and manage, you are likely to be paying the Ethereum blockchain to do so, and since you can only pay the blockchain in the Ether crytpocurrency, you are going to need to get some Ether. (Another translation for non-crypto-natives - don’t be blinded by teminology - when you hear the phrase “gas fees” for Ethereum, have your brain auto-translate to “transaction processing fees” - it’s the same thing).

This is going to happen. It’s going to happen not because some bored teenagers want it to happen. It’s going to happen because the asset management industry wants it to happen. (That’s why Larry Fink is such a zealot on the topic of Ethereum. )

Some Ways To Try To Make Money From The Rise Of The Ether Complex

Like any new departure in finance, the road to riches will be littered with ruined speculators. This trend is no different. Fortunes will be made and hearts will be broken and investors should manage their exposure and risks accordingly. But in essence these are the ways I believe opportunity lies.

  • Buy Ether, in its native form on crypto exchanges or (my personal preference) in fund-managed format, be that the BlackRock ETHA ETF or the older (and higher fee burden) ETHE from Grayscale.
  • Buy one or more of the new “Ether Treasury Companies”. This is a big name for an old concept, that of the investment trust. Names such as BitMine Immersion Technologies, SharpLink Gaming, BTCS and others are all raising capital to acquire increasing quanties of the Ether cryptocurrency. As with Bitcoin, supply of Ether is limited by design, so if it is being hoovered up you would expect the supply to restrict, the price to rise, and the benefits accrue to Ether Treasury Companies’ shareholders. You’ve seen this movie before in the shape of MicroStrategy (MSTR) which has been a more or less solo at-scale player doing this in Bitcoin. The vertical rise of MSTR stock has attracted wannabes; the two highest profile names to be involved in these Ether Treasury Companies are Tom Lee of FundStrat Advisors, and Peter Thiel, the well-known investor. They are now Chairman and a 9%+ shareholder respectively in BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR).

I Can’t Tell You What To Do.

You have to make your own decisions of course, but personally I have been building positions in:

  • ETHE (the Grayscale Ether ETF)
  • BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) - because Tom Lee and Peter Thiel, and it because the company now owns c. $1bn of Ether.
  • SharpLink Gaming (SBET) - the second-largest holder of Ether amongst the treasury companies.
  • Then a smattering of others including BTCS (BTCS), GameSquare Holdings (GAME) and BitDigital (BTBT).

If you are thinking about position sizing, my own view is that BMNR and SBET are the likely winners. They have the personalities aboard (Lee / Thiel in the case of the former, and cofounders of the Ethereum project in the case of the latter) and they are already placed 1 and 2 in the arms race to acquire increasing amounts of this scarce digital commodity.

But This Is All Nonsense

I don’t think it is. I think it’s a real thing. And I think that played correctly, excellent returns can be made, but this is nothing like owning the S&P500, so if you choose to play, you will need to manage risk differently to large index or major single-stock holdings. Volatility will be a feature not a bug; there will be consolidation and failure of the weak companies along the way; a global event may crater the price of Ether and take these stocks with it. My own view is the prize is worth it; but it’s not my first rodeo, I’ve been a technology investor my entire career, spanning from dot-com boom and bust through Internet 2.0, Web3, AI and a hundred other yo-yo themes.

Good luck if you choose to celebrate!

Alex King, Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 17 July 2025.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Zoetis: Poised For Growth Yet Priced For Decline (Rating Upgrade To Strong Buy)
data.description:
Background

Since its spin-off from Pfizer (PFE) in 2012, the New Jersey-based Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) has become the most dominant pharmaceutical company engaged in animal health. Approximately 2/3rd of its revenue is derived from treatments for pets, and the other 1/3rd from livestock. By a significant margin, they remain the largest player in this space.

I previously wrote on Zoetis for Seeking Alpha back in November 2024. It was a fairly deep dive into the company’s operations, assessing secular tailwinds, and its competitive moat, future growth potential, capital allocation/efficiency, management, and valuation. I would suggest those looking for more detail in terms of my thoughts on the company should read that article as well, linked >>here<<. It has been one of my most widely read articles on Seeking Alpha. With shares trading at around $174 at the time, and now hovering around $150, my buy rating has not aged well, however. I did acknowledge shares were fairly priced at the time, but there was clearly a premium on them. Back in November, the market cap was $79 million, the dividend yield was 0.99%, and the stock carried a forward P/E ratio of 28.5 and a PEG ratio of 3.2. Let’s compare these with today:

Not only has the stock become cheaper in a strict dollar sense, relative to its future earnings and future earnings growth, it is trading at a multi-year low. Rarely has it breached the historical low of 25 as a forward P/E ratio, but we’re sitting here today at around 24.

You’d think the decline would be due to deteriorating fundamentals, like falling revenue, declining margins, or market share losses, but the opposite is the case. Here is their revenue chart.

Margins have also continued to improve, showing no signs of decline.

And in terms of market share, let’s compare developments from 2022 to today. Below is a table that I compiled of the top 10 market players in the animal health industry, according to revenue.

What we can see is that their market share has increased during this period, from 27.8% in 2022 to 28.1% at the end of the last calendar year (2024). While market share growth has been stronger for IDEXX (IDXX) and Virbac (OTCPK:VRBCF), alongside the animal health segment of Boehringer Ingelheim – the largest privately-held company in this space – Zoetis is a much larger company, so we would not expect the same rates of growth and market share accrual as some of their smaller competitors.

Zoetis has also enjoyed steady and predictable EBITDA growth over the past 13 years, with more recent growth very much in line with their historical averages (see below).

Management has been able to translate this revenue and EBITDA growth to an even stronger EPS growth rate, partly due to share buybacks. Since 2015, when buybacks commenced, more than 10% of the total float has been repurchased, that is, over 50 million shares. The last quarter was the most aggressive in the company’s history in this regard. When we add the buyback yield to the dividend yield (currently 1.3%), the shareholder yield stands at 3.2%.

If we add the shareholder yield to the average EPS growth over the last 3 years (12 quarters), which stands at 9.23%, we get 12.43%, which would handily beat the S&P 500 (SP500) average over a long timeframe. This suggests good value.

So, we have a company that is #1 in its industry, growing market share year-over-year, enjoying rising revenue, EBITDA, and EPS, improving gross, operating, and net margins, with a decent shareholder yield including dividends and buybacks. Due to the company’s incredible track record for growth, it deserves to trade at a premium, yet its P/E ratio is now well below its 5-year average and in line with the S&P 500.

From these figures alone, it would seem that everything seems to be going right with it, yet shares have fallen from an all-time high of around $250 just before the COVID bubble burst at the end of 2021 (a drawdown of 40%), but also from a more recent high of around $200, which it reached several times in 2024. It is now range-bound, held up by strong support around $150. From a technical setup alone, shares could easily rebound to $200, representing a 33% gain from current prices.

However, I only utilize technical analysis in a simple way – looking for trading ranges, base and support levels – and only when the fundamentals line up. So, let’s look at the company’s growth prospects amidst recent developments and secular tailwinds to assess the risk-reward profile for Zoetis right now.

Growth: Recent Developments and Secular Tailwinds

Let’s look at Zoetis’s track record first. They have a history of innovative treatments across 7 major product categories: anti-infectives, dermatology, pain & sedation, diagnostics, parasiticides, vaccines, and other pharma. To date, they have introduced more than 2,000 products and innovations to the market in the last 12 years, including 17 blockbuster drugs (2 more since my last article in November), referring to those generating at least $100 million in sales.

Across several product categories, they remain dominant, though I note that since I last reported on Zoetis – referring to their Nov 2024 investor presentation – they have dropped from 2nd to 3rd place in swine treatments, and from 5th to 6th in poultry, but rose from 3rd to 2nd place in vaccines.

In their last earnings call, they reported 53.3% of their revenue from U.S. sales, and 46.7% from international sales. U.S. sales grew 2% year on year, while international revenue was flat. Operational growth was 7% internationally, but FX headwinds offset all this growth. FX headwinds were quite significant and felt from almost every international market. However, it should be noted that, internationally, only China and Mexico saw operational revenue decline – China at -20% and Mexico at -1% – but they represented only 2.7% and 1.7% of sales in total, respectively, so this reduction was largely immaterial. All other international markets saw growth, with only FX headwinds impacting revenue across the board.

While FX headwinds were significant in the last quarter, in my view they should not be counted upon as impactful over the long term, due to variations being a normal condition of having a large overseas business. In most markets, FX rates are range-bound and can provide a headwind as much as a tailwind in any given year or quarter. Long-term investors should consider this, in effect, “market noise” and instead focus on their organic (operational) growth rates and margins when assessing this company. Regarding its gross margin, as I mentioned earlier, Q1 2025 saw improvements in its US gross margin, from 81.3 to 83.2% and in its international gross margin, from 68.9 to 70%. This suggests they have pricing power driven by the large moat surrounding their business.

Indeed, across their leading treatments, they saw revenue growth. The Simparica franchise (including Simparica and Simparica Trio) recognized $367 million in revenue, up 19% from the same quarter in 2024. These ales represented 16.5% of total revenue, up from 14.4% in 2024. Simparica Trio remains the market-leading parasiticide for dogs, and recently (in April) was granted FDA approval for another indication: flea tapeworm infections.

Their key dermatology franchise also saw double-digit revenue growth. The two versions of Apoquel (regular and chewable) and Cytopoint combined recognized $360 million in Q1 2024, but $387 million in 2025, representing 10% growth. In Q1 2025, these three represented 17.4% of total revenue, up from 16.4% in Q1 2024. Currently, Apoquel remains the top-selling dermatology product in the US, and management estimates that 20 million dogs worldwide remain untreated or undertreated, which signals the drug’s potential market expansion driven by new patient growth.

Finally, their osteoarthritis (OA) pain franchise (including Librela and Solensia) recognized $147 million in sales for the quarter, versus $131 million the previous year (an annual growth rate of 15%). This franchise represented 6.7% of total revenue for the quarter, up from 6% the previous year. Zoetis remains #1 in the "pet pain" category, and management estimates that around 40% of dogs suffer from OA during their lifetime, but less than 20% receive treatment, which signals significant room for future growth.

To sustain their moat around these key products, they have over 5000 patents, of which slightly more than 60% are active. In the U.S., with patents typically lasting 20 years from the first approval date, it would appear that their pricing power for these medicines will last – for four of their five best-selling drugs – into the 2040s, and for Cytopoint until at least 2035.

Patent protection and their leading position across most of the approved indications should see revenue growth sustained for some time. However, also impacting their growth prospects, in a positive way, are broader secular tailwinds. These were explored in greater detail in my first analysis of the company, so I won’t repeat them, but here are the points in summary that remain just as relevant now as they did in November 2024:

  • Strong if not deepening human-animal bond, with a reported 86% of pet owners stating they would pay "whatever it takes" if their pet needed extensive veterinary care, according to the 2022 International Survey of Pet Owners and Veterinarians. The Millennial and Gen Z generations and those with higher-than-average incomes are fueling this trend, as are the increasing numbers of people who view pet ownership as positive for their own mental health.
  • Aging of pandemic-era pets. Pet ownership increased significantly during COVID in places like Australia (rising from 61-69% from 2019 to 2021), the UK (3.2 million pets were acquired during the first year of the pandemic, the BBC reported), and the US, where the Washington Post reported that nearly 1 in 5 households (23 million households) adopted a new pet during the pandemic. As these animals age, their healthcare costs will increase, creating a kind of baby boomer situation, seen from 10-15 years post-pandemic (so, roughly 2030-37).
  • Expanding global population will demand animal protein.

All of these tailwinds are long-term and point to sustained growth for Zoetis as the leading beneficiary due to its market leading position, but what kind of CAGR should we expect from this industry? Looking across a swathe of different market research companies, we see various growth projections for the animal health industry. The average across the six below is a 9.4% CAGR over the next 5-9 years. This seems reasonable and not too far off Zoetis’s own projections.

Historically, Zoetis has beaten these growth rates by about 3% a year (the difference between 5% and 8%), which means – if these growth rates are to be believed – they could, potentially, enjoy a 12% CAGR in organic growth alone, just in terms of operational revenue. When factoring in their rising profit margins and share buybacks, the EPS growth could be even higher than that, and when added to the modest dividend, could be closer to 15%. Factoring any quarters of FX tailwinds too, and the figure would be even better, but these should be considered a bonus rather than something that should be specifically planned for or expected, in my view. In any case, it’s all positive.

Management also cited some other compelling factors that could accelerate growth going forward. Alongside the new Simparica Trio indication (mentioned above) were several other recent news developments that would lead to increased revenues. Indeed, the company has a track record of medical advances, and this gives me confidence that they can be relied upon to continue leading the industry in these regards.

Given these organic and secular growth drivers, I have no reason to believe that Zoetis will not retain its market-leading position and enjoy sustained growth going forward. Let’s take a look at the company’s financials and summarize its valuation.

Company Financials

Zoetis has cash and short-term investments of $1.741 billion, among current assets worth $5.877 billion. Of its total assets of $14.098 billion, a significant $2.716 billion is from goodwill. If we subtract that amount (as it is a non-cash item that tends to skew the stats), they still have a significant amount greater than total liabilities, which are valued at $9.443 billion. Of this, they have little short-term debt, but long-term debt of $5.248 billion (down from $6.612 billion in 2020). They paid $274 million of interest on their total debt over the last twelve months, which is easily covered by their FCF of nearly $2.3 billion. This is good.

They have a current ratio of 1.74, a quick ratio of 0.92, and a debt/equity ratio of 1.46. While I’d love the first two figures to be higher and the second to be much lower – indeed, their balance sheet is good but not great – it is certainly manageable. Their strong and reliable future cash flow removes much of the risk here, so perhaps a more important metric to look for is how they allocate their cash for investing purposes. How are they creating value through their own investments?

The company has been able to increase their R&D spending over the years, yet also improve upon their high ROIC figure, which shows that the company is very good at allocating capital and investing for future growth. These increasing figures across the board are what investors want to see.

R&D spend has increased year-over-year but remained approximately 21.8% of operating expenses, in line with historical averages if not slightly elevated. In 2020, R&D represented 19.8%, and this increase is what you want to see from a biotech company, because this represents future revenue potential.

Zoetis has increased their dividends for 12 consecutive years and has a current payout ratio of just under 35%, so it seems secure and well covered by future cash flows. Given how much the stock price has fallen, the dividend (at over 1.3%) is no longer immaterial as it perhaps used to be. Its yield is at a 12-year high.

Conclusion

How should we rate Zoetis shares right now in terms of risk and reward? Firstly, compared to peers, it is easy to argue that the company looks expensive, even after its 40% drawdown from their all-time high in 2021. However, not only are we likely to see improving gross and net margins, but also EPS going forward. Its EPS growth rate over the past 3 years was around 9.2%, and I expect this to increase slightly.

Analysts seem to agree. According to Stock Analysis, the mean projected EPS growth for full-year 2025 is around 15% EPS growth, with about 10% for the following two years. With a forward PE of around 24, this brings its 1-year PEG ratio down below 2, which for a company of this quality, seems like an excellent deal. Indeed, while this figure might seem quite high in relation to other companies in its industry, Zoetis is the market leader and is growing market share with its wide moat. Just like so many other market leading companies across many industries with a wide moat, it might always seem that expensive.

Indeed, their historical PEG ratio has been quite high, much higher than I am normally comfortable with as an investment, but I also acknowledge that investors in effect pay up for a company’s excellent track record, as it implies security in their future earnings, thereby reducing risk. If management have proven themselves before, why not bet on them sustaining their trajectory of growth into the future?

As such, I would argue that Zoetis represents a Strong Buy at these prices. The company is continuing to compound at good rates, has a durable competitive advantage, and is basically recession-proof, given spending on pets is considered non-discretionary for the vast majority of pet owners. From a technical analysis perspective, Zoetis are at the bottom of a long-term support level, with a medium-term pop to around $200 quite likely from my perspective. I own ZTS shares at an average price of $163, and will look to add more at these levels given what I perceive to be long-term tailwinds for the company and its industry.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NVDX Aims To Deliver 2x Nvidia, Here's How I'd Use It (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Introduction

First things first, congrats to Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) for reaching $4T in market cap, now around 4.23T. The breakout star of the Magnificent 7 is now fully on top, notching the accomplishment of being the very first to touch the $4T mark in capital markets history.

Particularly, the most recent jump was brought on by a reversal of a ban on sales of certain chips to China. This is a massive boon for NVDA, and only helped solidify the bull run it was already on.

This kind of run-up has naturally brought in many speculators, and leveraged single-stock funds targeting NVDA have exploded. Many traders are lured in by the promise of excess returns on NVDA, which itself has been one of the best performing large-cap stocks in recent memory.

Enter the T-Rex 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF (BATS:NVDX). Its AUM fluctuates wildly, but is now back above $700M.

While NVDX isn't at an all-time high like NVDA is, that's because it's a tactical trading vehicles and less of a long-term investment. These kinds of leveraged ETFs are not considered suitable as long-term investments, but that doesn't mean they are worthless.

I mean, look at how NVDX has performed since its inception in late 2023. We are close to NVDX's all-time high. If you are bullish on NVDA, it may make sense to deploy some capital into NVDX as well, bolstering an NVDA position and adding some excess alpha if NVDA does run up.

Before I get into that, however, I should give a very clear warning about NVDX in particular.

Proceed With Caution

There are three warnings that I think investors should consider when looking at any daily-resetting leveraged fund:

1) The SEC notes:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs are typically designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that the performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

2) NVDX's prospectus says:

The Fund is designed to be utilized only by knowledgeable investors who understand the potential consequences of seeking daily leveraged (2X) investment results, understand the risks associated with the use of leverage and are willing to monitor their portfolios frequently. The Fund is not intended to be used by, and is not appropriate for, investors who do not intend to actively monitor and manage their portfolios. For periods longer than a single day, the Fund will lose money if [NVDA's] performance is flat, and it is possible that the Fund will lose money even if [NVDA's] performance increases over a period longer than a single day. An investor could lose the full principal value of his/her investment within a single day if the price of [NVDA] falls by more than 50% in one trading day.

3) This review of MSTX, the "Case Study For Bad Leveraged ETFs" can be viewed as a cautionary tale.

Deploying NVDX Tactically

So, how to actually use these kinds of funds?

Tactically, of course!

That means deploying capital into NVDX at opportune times, usually before you expect NVDA to go on a run. That's much easier said than done, of course, which is why these funds have warnings attached to them.

If you choose the wrong timing, you may end up with horrific drawdowns and underperformance on the way back up. It's all about entry points.

That last chart goes back to the last market top in February, before our current one. What if you had bought NVDX down at the bottom? This next chart starts at the April lows.

So if timing is everything, then it stands to reason that we should work out when would be a good time to enter NVDA.

NVDA Technical Analysis

The way I have learned best to handle these kinds of situations is with technical analysis. This is how I identify entry points for these kinds of tactical trades, and how I would decide when to deploy NVDX over NVDA, to modulate my exposure and potentially add excess returns during bull runs of the stock.

Here is the current one-year chart with my personal trading indicators overlays, legend at the top of the image to show what I use:

Note that I've been seeing the same patten in a lot of these kinds of charts, and I'm bullish on the Nasdaq because of it. I see this pattern in NVDA too, and it makes me bullish, despite the run-up to all-time highs.

Looking at the exponential moving averages ("EMAs"), we have a classic bullish continuation set-up, with the 20-day (blue), 50-day (yellow), 100-day (purple), and 200-day (maroon) all in line atop each other.

Current momentum indicators ((MACD, PPO)) are bullish, although tepid, but that is to be expected as we get to these levels and bounce religiously off the Bollinger bands (white). The bands could be the cause of a slight pullback, as they tend to act as a strict ceiling unless we see more positive macro catalysts like the China sales ban lifting, which could send the stock surging regardless of the technicals.

The largest red flag is the relative strength index ("RSI"), which is nearing overbought and overheated. A 77 isn't the end of the world, but it does signal that there may be little strength left in the stock for a minute. This could signal a coming pullback, which would present a possible buying opportunity.

Before we get into where I see possible entry points for NVDA, it should be of note that I am assuming that this is also a possible entry point for NVDX, or a combination of NVDA and NVDX. This is an ETF that could perform very well over short bursts of time, and could lead to excess returns if traded tactically. This could be a way for NVDA investors to deploy some extra capital during opportune times, or a way for investors to create synthetic exposure to NVDA through NVDX's returns.

Conservative Entry Point

The conservative trader (oxymoron?) would wait for a firm pullback, down to the EMA support. The current levels to watch, although this will likely change as the days go on, are the 20 EMA at $159 or the 50 EMA at $146.

Buy signal confirmation would be:

  • Increasing volume after bouncing off the EMA with a bullish candle.
  • Bullish MACD and PPO, still in the green.
  • An RSI pullback to the 60s or 50s (ideally).

Aggressive Entry Point

The aggressive trader may be buying in as we speak, trying to ride momentum. After all, we are still in a position to continue the current uptrend. While the upper band of the Bollinger bands is restrictive, NVDA has a history of busting out of them during quick run-ups, and current bulls should see no problem with that pattern continuing.

There may be a pullback next week, based on the current setup, and that would provide the aggressive buying opportunity even if we don't see a full retest of supports.

Buy signal confirmation would be:

  • A small red candle, with a long wick that indicates demand.
  • Support holding at the 10 EMA (not on my chart, but at the $171 mark).
  • Enter on the break above the previous small red candle.
  • Watch for RSI 80+, "oversold."

Risk Management

Of course, because of the fickle nature of these kinds of ETFs like NVDX, it's prudent for traders — note that I didn't write investors, it feels odd to use that term when discussing tactically deploying leveraged ETFs — to keep strict risk management rules when handling funds like NVDX.

This is a fund that I would use sparingly, and only for those moments where one has very high conviction in NVDA.

It could be a very good addition to one's NVDA's position in the place of an options position, or to bolster an existing NVDA position that one doesn't want to add to, per se, for a short-term trade.

Someone bullish on NVDA could easily use NVDX to take a big swing with a smaller amount of capital, given its leveraged nature. That being said, there is excess risk in leveraged funds if you're wrong.

NVDX does have an options market, although it is less liquid than NVDA's, of course. This means one could cap their downside on both with protective puts, but a collar or other risk-first options strategy on a leveraged ETF like NVDX could provide a better risk/reward ratio than NVDA, given one gets the right fills on their options.

Remember that position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in this ETF. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-stock ETFs such as this. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Conclusion

Ultimately, we're likely still on a bull path for Nvidia, and that could open up opportunities for traders to deploy capital into the T-REX 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF (NVDX) as well.

This ETF could offer a great opportunity for traders to capitalize on NVDA's bullish moment, which it has in spades currently, as per its chart. In the coming days, we may see potential entry points for NVDA and NVDX, or a combo of the two.

Of course, prudent risk management is necessary, but traders may be able to use NVDA's technical chart to position themselves in NVDX, and take advantage of the world's largest company's bull run.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Block: Rally Isn't Finished Yet - Don't Bail Now
data.description:
Block: Best Days Over?

Investors in Block, Inc. (XYZ) have likely assessed the overall trajectory of the stock to be an enigma at the moment. Fintech stocks like XYZ or even that of PayPal (PYPL) have failed to recover their late 2024 or early 2025 highs, even as the financial sector peers (XLF) notched record closures in July.

Questions like the following have likely afflicted the investing psychology of XYZ and its peers, given the underlying reliance on the payments ecosystem (one- or two-sided).

  • Is the market concerned about whether the best days for massive fintech growth as over?
  • While the economy has remained resilient, could we see tariff-related impacts start percolating through the rest of the economy in the second half?
  • VCs have pivoted their attention toward the surging AI companies, directing their attention away from early-stage fintech companies. Does it suggest that we should expect markedly slower growth ahead "after a decade of supersized venture capital investments?"
  • Concerns about profitability margins facing dilution risks amidst the heightened competitive fintech climate as big tech encroaches into the payments space, integrating their platform ecosystem.

Despite that, I'm still rather pleased (but not ebullient) about XYZ's relative performance to the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) since my previous writeup. I expounded on the opportunity to seize the initiative after its previous battering, although Block's earnings release led to a further downdraft (initially).

Accordingly, XYZ suffered another hammering when it delivered its first quarter earnings scorecard. However, investors subsequently brushed that skepticism aside, as the stock has also gained traction with the broad market ascent. Therefore, it has also helped XYZ achieve a respectable outperformance against the market, although it remains far below (almost 30%) the zenith that it attained late last year.

Hence, how could Block turn its fortunes around as it battles against intensified macroeconomic concerns. Since it's categorized alongside its financial sector peers, the market will conceivably expect a solid earnings follow through in Block's upcoming Q2 earnings release after seeing the robust scorecard delivered by the major banks for Q2. With Block's guidance for the full year already lowered, could the initial caution earlier in the year help set a lower bar for the company to outperform.

Particularly, since Wall Street has already arguably baked in substantive circumspection in their outlook. Bullish market prognosticators might also contend that the pessimists overstated their early bearishness? And for growth related financial plays like Block, having a second half growth inflection could help to maintain its fledgling recovery, and help close the bifurcation from its 2024 peak.

Block: Anticipating A 2026 Growth Inflection

Interestingly, analysts don't expect the growth deceleration to persist through 2026. Also, the company's ability to continue gaining operating leverage as it scales isn't anticipated to suffer a structural impact. While the peak growth days before 2021 aren't likely to return, I think there are several promising growth vectors that we still expect the Block's integrated two-sided ecosystem to be capable of delivering to help meet the projections.

While there's no doubt that XYZ is still valued at a premium over its financial median, it can still justify its valuation as it scales up from its relatively low margins previously, demonstrating the sustainability of its business model, notwithstanding the intensified competitive dynamics.

And for that, I believe we have to accord credit to Block management for attempting to build in a "macro buffer" as it tempered its 2025 outlook. I think the conservatism is apt, as it affords more bandwidth for the company to surpass the lowered bar in the second half.

Notably, management highlighted several pivotal growth vectors that could shift the bullish narrative back into the stock. Keen followers of XYZ should have noted the commentary:

  • Continuing on its push to drive borrow expansion through Cash App, aiming to monetize the hard-earned 57M monthly transacting actives that have remained relatively consistent over the past 12 months. Hence, with acquisitive growth clearly stagnated, the crucial pillars in driving underlying growth will depend on broadening the products range within the active cohort base.
  • Solidifying its traction in Afterpay (BNPL platform). The less onerous regulatory headwinds on the BNPL platform should help alleviate some of that previous pressure. However, it remains to be seen whether integrating BNPL into debit cards could spur a more robust growth inflection, as the target segment is still predicated on the less wealthy Gen Z spending cohort. Yet, the 7M active consumers embedded within Cash App Pay could still help drive growth optionality for the segment.
  • Merchant ecosystem through Square should be strengthened as it bolsters the platform further through its hardware-software integration. Also, the move up to more larger merchants should be noted, as management targets more "sales-led" initiatives to secure more significant deals. Yet, I must also caution that moving away from self-serve (a lower touch approach) could also bring along less predictable sales cycle momentum, and plausibly lumpier revenue accretion.

Hence, I believe that the market will likely examine whether these initiatives could lead to a re-rating in its guidance for the rest of the year after being downgraded in Q1. Based on the remarkable recovery from its April nadir, I expect optimism of uplifted projections is priced into valuation.

Despite that, fears about a sharp slowdown due to Trump's tariff turmoil seem overstated, notwithstanding the highest overall tariff rates in recent memory. Yet, consumer spending has remained incredibly resilient, and e-commerce growth remains skewed toward the upside. Therefore, the growth vectors should continue scaling up as Block moves further into profitability growth even as topline growth decelerated.

Buy Or Sell XYZ Stock?

Yet, some concerns about XYZ's price action relative to its XLF peers cannot be fully discounted. Clearly, the implosion on fintech stocks has spooked financial sector investors as the downtrending XYZ/XLF lingers.

Bouts of optimism lifting XYZ's performance have occurred from time to time. However, the lower-highs and lower-lows price structures corroborate reservations that the market seems to place as the growth profile in XYZ has cooled substantially from its heydays.

Hence, even though it's still rated as an A+ growth stock relative to its financial peers, we must be careful into buying into momentum spikes, as its forward adjusted earnings multiple of 25x is significantly higher than the sector median of about 11x.

Nevertheless, I think the mean-reversion opportunity doesn't appear to have fully completed its current upswing. Hence, XYZ's bullish thesis baked on an already lowered 2025 outlook may continue its outperformance through 2026.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
